title: Insulating cold water pipes to prevent freezing and condensation
subtitle: Foam lagging for cold supply pipes in unheated spaces
excerpt: Cold water pipes in unheated roof spaces, garages, and external walls can freeze in UK winters. Fitting foam lagging to all unheated pipe runs is a morning's job. The same lagging prevents summer condensation on cold pipes in humid spaces.
sourceNotes: Energy Saving Trust pipe lagging guidance. gov.uk guidance on protecting pipes in cold weather. Water Regulations Advisory Scheme (WRAS) guidance on pipe protection.

content.text: Cold water supply pipes in a roof space sit just above the insulation. They stay above freezing because warm air from the house below rises past them. Once loft insulation is laid at floor level (the right place in an unoccupied loft), the roof space above is colder than it was before. The heat the pipes used to get is now held by the insulation below.
on all pipe runs in the roof space is the necessary companion to loft insulation.
All cold water pipes in the loft: supply to the cold water cistern, overflow pipes, and the cistern itself if it is not already insulated.
Pipes in an unheated garage: any supply pipe routed through a garage that is not heated is at risk.
Pipes that run on an outside wall. A cold supply pipe fixed to an outside wall in an unheated utility room or cellar.
Pipes under a suspended floor near an airbrick where cold air circulates directly.

Measure the pipe diameter before buying foam lagging. Standard UK pipe sizes are 15 mm and 22 mm (copper and plastic, both common). Foam lagging is sold by bore diameter: a 15 mm bore lagging fits 15 mm pipe; 22 mm bore fits 22 mm pipe. For severe exposure (a north-facing unheated wall or a loft in a very exposed location), 25 mm wall thickness foam gives better protection than the standard 13 mm at modest additional cost.
Cut the foam lagging to length with a utility knife. Cut the mitres for elbows and T-junctions at 45 degrees so the cut faces meet cleanly.
Press the foam sleeve over the pipe along its pre-cut slit. The foam expands to grip the pipe.
Tape all joints and mitre cuts with the self-adhesive tape supplied with the lagging or with a standard duct tape. An untaped joint is a thermal weak point.

At the cistern: fit a proprietary pre-formed insulation jacket designed for cold water cisterns. The jacket insulates the lid as well as the sides. Don't leave the base uninsulated: the coldest air in a roof space is at floor level.

In a humid summer, cold water pipes in an unlagged utility room or kitchen can weep condensation. The pipe surface is below the dewpoint of the warm humid air. Foam lagging prevents the air reaching the cold surface. This is the same lagging material and installation method, applied for the opposite reason: in summer, the foam keeps moisture out; in winter, it keeps heat in.
